summary:
The Asparn-Schletz massacre site (also known as Schletz) is a Late Linear Pottery culture (LBK) settlement in Lower Austria, dated to approximately 5000–4800 BCE, where the skeletal remains of at least 67 individuals were found in the outer ditch of the enclosed settlement, showing clear evidence of violent death. Many skulls bore traumatic injuries consistent with blunt-force weapon blows, and numerous individuals showed signs of arrow wounds. The skeletal remains represent a non-random demographic profile — primarily adult males and adult females, with an underrepresentation of older adults and children — suggesting that the latter may have been abducted rather than killed.

The Asparn-Schletz site represents one of the clearest archaeological demonstrations of [[event:1585|large-scale prehistoric warfare]] in central Europe. The scale of the event — dozens of people killed in a single episode — and the context of an enclosed settlement suggest a planned assault on a community, possibly by a neighboring group competing for agricultural land or other resources. The evidence from Schletz, alongside the [[event:1583|Talheim Death Pit]], has transformed scholarly understanding of Neolithic violence from isolated incidents to evidence of systematic, potentially organized conflict.

The massacre at Asparn-Schletz occurred near the end of the LBK cultural horizon, a period associated in the archaeological record with signs of increasing social stress, settlement fortification, and intergroup conflict. These patterns suggest that the rapid expansion of LBK farming communities across central Europe created competitive pressures that could erupt into violent conflict at the community or regional level.
